speaker: interviewer
text: Berek, would you like to introduce your family to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. Rose, my wife. John, my eldest son. Michael, where are you? MICHAEL (SON): Here. Here, the second son. And my grandson, Ramesh, the oldest one. Joshua, [INTERPOSING VOICES] the one-- ROSE (WIFE): Second. MICHAEL (SON): Middle one. Middle one. And what is your name? [LAUGHTER] ROBBIE (GRANDSON): Robbie. Robbie. Is that right? Robbie, the youngest one.

speaker: interviewee
text: Would anyone like to say a few words here? JOHN (SON): A few words-- I think Michael, say a few words now.

speaker: interviewee
text: MICHAEL (SON): Now, a few words, it's marvelous that you could come and do this. And we thank Mr. Spielberg for putting this together for us. And I'm very pleased that my father and mother have been-- are here with us today, experiencing this, considering everything that they've been through. And we love them very, very much. And we've learned very, very much from them. My father has always led by example. And he's a man of principle. Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: MICHAEL (SON): And what can I tell you?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] Everything-- everything as it is, is-- is a bit of a miracle in a way.

speaker: interviewer
text: And here we are.

speaker: interviewee
text: Or good luck. MICHAEL (SON): Or good luck if you like, of course. Good luck can't be overlooked. JOHN (SON): And it's good that this is-- this is happening. Because it'll be sort of in color that this really actually did happen. And people went through all that. And nobody can actually deny that this has happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: But it's affected them in many different ways, you know? There's been-- it's made him a little bit more-- you know, in-- in their life, a little bit careful about things, you know? Not-- not as-- not as-- not big risk takers, you know? They have a very simple, good, quiet life. And you know, the real, true values probably, you know? Not showoffs and nothing like, you know, trying to impress the neighbors or anything like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: MICHAEL (SON): They-- they probably worked out that they took a few risks already. JOHN (SON): Yeah. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: MICHAEL (SON): Already.

speaker: interviewer
text: Berek, do you have anything you'd like to say to your grandchildren? A message for them in the future?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, I am very proud of them. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] What I have to say, they-- they see how we're living. And there's a-- that's a cardinal example, [? what ?] they want to-- to follow, the type of life we have been living for 57 years. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: They have their example. OK. That's very nice. Thank you. MICHAEL (SON): Son? Anybody else have anything to say? MICHAEL (SON): Could I add this little bit? Yeah. MICHAEL (SON): Sorry about that. Just comes a little bit later. Again, we've learned from our parents' courage and survival skills. And there are many things that take too long to go into.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I think the Schindler experience, in this case, serves to show the world what could be done when people want to do it. And perhaps the greatest lesson we might be able to learn from this sort of experience is that it's not actually the banality of evil that's the problem. It's the banality of conformity that creates disasters.

speaker: interviewer
text: The human capacity to want to conform, that would be something that-- that I might add to that. Yes, darling?

speaker: interviewee
text: ROSE (WIFE): Maybe Ramesh and Joshua--

speaker: interviewee
text: MICHAEL (SON): Joshua and Ramesh? ROSE (WIFE): --want to say a few words. Ramesh?

speaker: interviewee
text: MICHAEL (SON): Speak. ROSE (WIFE): A few words. RAMESH (GRANDSON): Oh-- ROSE (WIFE): A few words. RAMESH (GRANDSON): I just think, my grandparents, they're pretty amazing people. They've changed with the times. They're pretty, like-- you could say, they're nice people just to be around. They're not really the sort of grandparents that you sort of want to get away from. They're sort of the people that sort of-- they draw other people towards them. But they don't draw-- they draw a certain type of people. They draw from their friends and the people they associate with. You can tell there's a lot of respect there. And they're very loyal and honest. And just everything about their lives is very sort of wholesome. That's the sort of feeling you get. It's a nice feeling. JOSHUA (GRANDSON): I can basically just agree with Ramesh, and just reiterate that. That's sort of-- basically, yeah. He's just got it pretty much all in a nutshell there.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, I'd like to thank you all for taking the time to come and do this. ROSE (WIFE): Robbie, you want to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: JOHN (SON): Come on. I think this is your chance. ROSE (WIFE): That he don't like us at all. [LAUGHTER] ROBBIE (GRANDSON): No, I do.

speaker: interviewee
text: ROSE (WIFE): Yeah?

speaker: interviewee
text: ROBBIE (GRANDSON): I'd just like to say thank you. Because you've always been there for me. And yeah, I know where to come if I need somebody. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: JOHN (SON): Says it all. OK, just wait one second. JOHN (SON): OK. Berek, Presser, tape four. We just had your family in, because they had to leave. So I know it's hard to go back from family back to-- to the wartime. But you were telling me about working for Schindler and 1,000 of the Jews working for Schindler were taken away from the--

speaker: interviewee
text: And they're all about-- end of-- oh, it was 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Where were they taken to, you said?

speaker: interviewee
text: The gossip was that they are going to Mauthausen. We couldn't prove anything. Only but after the war, I met some people who-- who survived the camp. They tell me what is [? real ?] going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So these people didn't come back?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not--

speaker: interviewer
text: To the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not many of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not many of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of information were you getting from outside of the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, because we having such a connection with the Polish people on the floor of the-- factory-- factory, we know about everything, what was going on. From them I would find out there was the uprising in the ghetto, Warsaw. And from them who have information that-- that Auschwitz is stinking with [? a ?] body who was burned. From them I have all the information, what we-- we need. Not because they-- they want to offense us. Just so in a way, they want to warn us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they tell you about the Warsaw ghetto uprising?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's hardly to believe. But once one of the worker where I was working outside in the last place of-- before we had been relocated, he told me I heard the Warsaw-- in Warsaw is uprising in ghetto. And they are shooting. And they have got more information. It was hardly to believe. But because we have a little bit of experience in ghetto of-- in Cracow, there was small group of young people who have been heading some-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] --kind of association. I couldn't tell you there was a right view or left view or anything. Anyhow, there was the young people. I know that one of them was shooting at the-- on the cafe in the street [INAUDIBLE], in Cracow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Another was shot in [INAUDIBLE], because the [INAUDIBLE] [? guy ?] seemed to have the information about him. What was his name? I couldn't remember now. But I knew his brothers very well.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so they had been traveling. The young people had been travelling between-- between the ghetto in district of Cracow, or something of this kind. And [? his ?] father have connection. But most of them had been finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: From the Cracow ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the Cracow ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had news?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because that's before they liquidated the Cracow ghetto. We would have to take another year before even [? seeing ?] more that was happening.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did you have access to newspapers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only there was one newspaper-- Cracow [INAUDIBLE] Cracow Daily-- Daily, something of this kind.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what type of--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was German-- German, in German. Only-- only in German. No-- no-- no Polish paper.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did you ever have visits from the-- from Germans, to the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we had quite a few of them. When-- when Schindler decided to-- to do something for the Wehrmacht, by this time, we have one or two inspections of people who have to write whether we are able to do something for them. And Schindler was going there, introduce me [? as ?] the foreman, [? as ?] [? someone, ?] [? with ?] [? kindness, ?] this way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they know you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did you ever hear of anybody trying to escape from Schindler's factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was only one case, what Schindler was-- know about. There was a dentist who was working in his room. He's supposed to attended-- he was technician. But he has the-- the right in Poland, before the war, to-- to make everything with teeth. Not only the-- so he was making money. He was-- he was talking though, to the Polish people, that he want to escape. He has money and would pay for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I don't know how. But Schindler knows him. He has everywhere-- everywhere, somebody. He was very gentle and very cooperative. But so long is somebody now is not pushing his hand into his pocket. And the responsibility will be on him, you know? Or of us when somebody escape.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was a case, Schindler went to [? Warsaw ?] for a kind of party. But he want to find out what's happened before the [INAUDIBLE], what will that be. And he came about seven o'clock in the morning. He was completely tired. He was very good drinker-- oh, a very good-- he'd have to have it-- a fellow who needs-- needed a whole lot of spirit to-- to be drunk.

speaker: interviewer
text: He didn't-- he was not drunk. He was gray, you know? Tired, gray. And he came over about six or seven o'clock in the morning.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it's-- that must have been before seven o'clock. Because I was night shift. And I was still in work. And myself, Mr. [? Sheck, ?] who was foreman in other shift, Mr. Bankier, and a few of those people. And Schindler had come over and told me, everything is all right. They have to be [INAUDIBLE] [? long. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: But the people who I need, I have reason they will stay until we advocate the-- how do you call it? Advocate? Evacuate.

speaker: interviewee
text: Evacuate the-- the factory to the-- to the Brinnlitz. And in-- in between he-- he told us, unfortunately, I-- I get-- I must send people who I think are [? through ?] [? protecting us. ?] I mean, they want to run away.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he told us one who I know, exactly, is the dentist. And we couldn't send any more. By this time, in a group of people who was standing, a lawyer, young lawyer-- his father was Israeli-- [? Bulva, ?] his name was. What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Bulva. ?] And in front of our whole-- our-- everybody, he asked Schindler such a-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --idiotic question in front of us all, that his sister is living in Switzerland. Because he lost contact with her, because she-- the Polish people didn't want-- he want the sister to write to Schindler. Schindler didn't answer anything. He-- he turn away and he disappeared. And next day, Mr. [? Bulva, ?] the-- the dentist, was sent to the [INAUDIBLE]. Probably they had been executed. I couldn't tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did you ever see Schindler get angry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not with the-- Jewish people. He was angry with the Polish people who were-- who had been stealing whatever they can, whatever they can. But they had to make a living. And the-- the-- pots and everything else was easy to sell. You get a very good price. And I-- and I told you, I explained to you, he was very good one, how long you don't put your-- your-- your hand to his pocket.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: When the new hall was built and new machine arrived, I and four of Polish people [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] went. I was sent to help, to [? install ?] the machine. And I believe the other-- the other thing what Schindler has in mind, that when I would be over there, they couldn't steal. But they find the excuse. When they want something to steal, he told me, Berek, go to the factory. And they were doing whatever they wanted. I don't want-- one of them was special. And he goes, I don't want you to-- to be-- [? because ?] what do you have [? within you? ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were very loyal.

speaker: interviewer
text: At what time did you realize that you were making mines? You mentioned it earlier. But when was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, very, very  late. Mainly for the mines, casting for mine-- the mine is very big one, tall one. There's only one small casting. Later they put explosives and so on. We had-- we had been doing only the casting of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And when did you find out what they were being used for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only what I told you, mentioned that I approached the man who was from Wehrmacht, who was overseeing the phase of production. When it was-- there was-- saying that we have--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. When-- when was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Four-- four-- four-- four steps with-- with one.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's at about end of '42 or before Christmas. Because-- because I was excused at-- because the Christmas times, [? fortunately, ?] were given something, as a day off.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did-- did the idea of sabotaging the mine ever cross your mind?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody make any sabotaging in there. We-- we haven't got the production. But nobody would, here, sabotage. You know, everything-- everything is coming from the people who have been with Schindler only for months. There was no sabotage. Because we'd-- we'd-- we want to do the best, what we can. But unfortunately, it was not working.

speaker: interviewer
text: What wasn't working?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because you know, they were like, they [? explain ?] [? you, ?] the old [? wool ?] of the-- of the casting have to be exactly the same with a-- with a difference, what can be allowed. And my wife was one of that one who was-- who was putting that one on the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Calibrator, yeah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Calibers, yeah, Caliber and the thickness of the holes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So because it wasn't thick enough, the mines weren't working?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. They-- because one side was two millimeter, the other one, 1 and 1/2 millimeter. They couldn't be. There was something [? when they're done. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did you have any idea that the Allied forces were getting closer to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the-- you have still, the same story. The Polish people have been-- have contacted when they didn't speak to everybody. But as I told you, I was in good connection. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Mr. [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE], was always informing what-- what was going on whenever he-- paid his dues. And when it was the Russian officer began to stop on the river Seine, and you-- you couldn't-- you shouldn't have information. You see what's going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Everybody's running. The cows were such that-- that they-- they ran by with a family when everything was again safe, you know? Or-- or the-- the-- once they've been looting and [? sent ?] everything [? away, they ?] [? sat ?] [? there a minute. ?] So how-- how far away was that from where you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, according to-- to my knowledge, they had been about 200 kilometers from Cracow. Or maybe 300. I don't know how long it is, how far it is to [INAUDIBLE]. I don't-- I don't exactly know. But about 200, 250 kilometers from Cracow.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. How did the Allied advance affect your work in your factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: In no way. We have got everyone alarm. No, no, I couldn't-- I couldn't remember nothing of this kind in Cracow.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK. Was your factory evacuated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in October-- September, October, started to evacuate the-- Under the [? fundament ?] of the big press, to prepare a route for them, to put that in there. He loaded in [? a ?] [? few. ?] I--

speaker: interviewer
text: September of which year?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And where were you going?

speaker: interviewee
text: According to-- to Schindler, we are going to Brinnlitz. But we have-- we don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is-- where is that, Brinnlitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Brinnlitz is Sudetenland. This was in north of Czechoslovakia, the border with Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What did Schindler say to you about what was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you-- you-- you couldn't exactly say. But the facts will-- will have to come over. It's not exactly answer in one way. But I can give you an example. When there were the cows, there was another chap, who we call him [? Avante. ?] He was once in-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --in the front with-- with Italian battalion. And his one word was [? avante. ?] And-- but he was very friendly. And he try-- because of the cows that [INAUDIBLE], we have a big pool with water hose where they would [? bombard ?] it. They-- they will take the water to-- to extend it to the fire or something.

speaker: interviewee
text: He tell everybody who want-- who want to-- have a swim. Later, he ask us, can we arrange some soccer here? There was quite a few good players in soccer, much younger than me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we have the [INAUDIBLE]. He was the [? theater. ?] And it was-- we have been having a good time spent in Sunday, also having people working. But there was not production only doing something with the machine to undo it, doing [? pen ?] parts and so on. And that was free, that you have a very good time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was-- was there-- was there any religious activity in-- amongst the workers at the camp, at the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: As I told you, we have an-- one rabbi. But he was not very active. And his--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there--

speaker: interviewee
text: What was his name? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Levertov.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was he from?

speaker: interviewee
text: From-- from some Cracow, where. I didn't know him. And he was, after the war, living in-- in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Was there any-- any events to mark the holidays, the high holidays, or-- that you can remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only few people have been observing something. One of them was a butcher, who didn't touch any soup with the meat. Funny, Mr. Levertov was eating everything. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: And you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would-- they didn't have enough for me. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So you-- Schindler told you you were being evacuated to Czechoslovakia. Where did you actually go?

speaker: interviewee
text: We go to-- to Mauthausen, very big experience.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you arrive there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't tell. It was-- I believe we went by-- on 15 of November. And we arrive one night later, we [? don't ?] [? know. ?] And there was something very unusual. Because we have not prepared [? to that. ?] We didn't have the experience. There was some-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --gutters. You were to sit down and-- and-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Make some-- make there. What they want to find was you have got some gold or everything in the, you know-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, the back of-- how-- how you call it? In hiding.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hidden.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was after your gold?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was fighting you for that, for the gold?

speaker: interviewee
text: The SS.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And what happened when you arrived in Mauthausen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, after this, what I told you, we have to undress and waiting because the people who are going to-- they're washing. This a little bit I'll explain. I was doing everything to be back-- in back-- back. Because the people are going inside and  nobody was going out. So I-- I-- I was maybe even thinking that is the last what-- what we have. But finally, I have to go. And I find out funny things. For 1,000 people, about-- where they had been, there was only one towel. [LAUGHTER] One towel there. yeah. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They had been shaving us from top to the bottom, with everything, private part as well. One of the-- only people who-- one of the who doesn't want to, listen, don't. Because they was very blind, the-- the-- shaving. He tell me unless you sharpen it, I will not let-- let you do. He was-- he was a surgeon, doctor, doctor-- I don't know. Anyhow, he was very-- very well off before the war. And he was young. And they-- they-- they sharpen the knife. And later they--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who-- who was doing the shaving?

speaker: interviewee
text: The probably Ukrainian. But they had been asking what's happening? What's going on? Whether-- whether-- how is it going? Because they haven't got any contact with the outside world. [? We'll ?] [? help ?] [? you. ?] [? And ?] [? we-- we aren't ?] talking [? a lot. ?] Mm, later we are going you know, to dry yourself. You couldn't dry with that one-- anyhow, we would squash it and dry it a little bit. And later was a doctor who was examining you, if you are enough health or something, or to-- to go to the Schindler factory. I have an accident in Poland. No, that wasn't accident. There was a scratch of-- of my whole [? one ?] here. And somehow didn't heal. And it was still open. It's rotten, stinking. And there was-- I was standing behind Dr. [? Blasberg. ?] My wife told you about that man. He was a doctor. He was once a Chief of Jewish Hospital in Cracow. A very good physician, but not young. He was very-- having very nice life. And I was [? scheming. ?] I told him listen, Doctor, you have to help me. Because I have dressing. And I undressed.

speaker: interviewee
text: It is stinking, you know? Rotten. So he told me, I'll try. And he, well, he approached the-- the doctor. He told him he's a doctor. And from this, the way he was speaking, the doctor, he went in his study. You know, we-- in Vienna. In Vienna? So the doctor asked him, who was your professor? Oh, he is my close friend. So that was very good, doctor. He was [? kind ?] [? to ?] [? him. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was treating him very well. And when I come over, he told me, he is only doing the dressing. He scratch himself. Go.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, stop here. We'll change the tape.
